title: Running inside (privileged) docker fails the first time, works subsequently
body:
### Issue Description

Running podman inside a docker container fails with the following messages the first time:

```
WARN[0002] Failed to add conmon to cgroupfs sandbox cgroup: creating cgroup path /libpod_parent/conmon: enabling controller cpuset: write /sys/fs/cgroup/libpod_parent/cgroup.subtree_control: no such file or directory 
Error: OCI runtime error: crun: the requested cgroup controller `pids` is not available
```

Redoing exactly the same command immediately afterwards still results in a warning:

```
WARN[0000] Failed to add conmon to cgroupfs sandbox cgroup: creating cgroup path /libpod_parent/conmon: enabling controller io: write /sys/fs/cgroup/libpod_parent/cgroup.subtree_control: no such file or directory 
```

but works fine otherwise.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Here is the full command sequence sufficient to reproduce the problem:

```console
# docker run -it --rm --privileged fedora:41
[root@bc4fe5e37291 /]# dnf install -y podman
...extensive dnf output snipped...
[root@bc4fe5e37291 /]# podman run --rm -it alpine
Resolved "alpine" as an alias (/etc/containers/registries.conf.d/000-shortnames.conf)
Trying to pull docker.io/library/alpine:latest...
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob da9db072f522 done   | 
Copying config 63b790fccc done   | 
Writing manifest to image destination
WARN[0002] Failed to add conmon to cgroupfs sandbox cgroup: creating cgroup path /libpod_parent/conmon: enabling controller cpuset: write /sys/fs/cgroup/libpod_parent/cgroup.subtree_control: no such file or directory 
Error: OCI runtime error: crun: the requested cgroup controller `pids` is not available
[root@bc4fe5e37291 /]# podman run --rm -it alpine
WARN[0000] Failed to add conmon to cgroupfs sandbox cgroup: creating cgroup path /libpod_parent/conmon: enabling controller io: write /sys/fs/cgroup/libpod_parent/cgroup.subtree_control: no such file or directory 
/ # head -n1 /etc/issue
Welcome to Alpine Linux 3.20 
```

Running it again works in the same way, i.e. complains about `io` cgroup controller (and _not_ `pids` any more) but still works.

### Podman in a container

Yes

### Privileged Or Rootless

Privileged

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

The host machine is an almost default installation of Debian 12 (Bookworm) on standard x86-64 hardware.

### Additional information

This is 100% reproducible.

comments.body:
you need to create a sub-cgroup and move podman there.  If there are processes running in the root cgroup, then podman cannot create sub-cgroups.

Podman has some logic to move itself to a child cgroup when it detects that it is running in the root cgroup, but there could be other processes left behind

Thanks, following your hint I've found out that just doing this:

```
[root@cedbd110d13a /]# mkdir /sys/fs/cgroup/init
[root@cedbd110d13a /]# echo 1 > /sys/fs/cgroup/init/cgroup.procs
```

i.e. moving the initial bash process into a different cgroup, allows podman to work without errors nor warnings. The workaround risks being a bit more complicated in my real use case, but at least I think I understand the problem now: the initial error is due to `/sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control` starting out as empty and podman failing to write to it because the root cgroup already contains some processes (PID 1, which is bash in this case). Somehow, however, the file still changes because after the first attempt to run podman it has `cpuset cpu pids`. And this is why it succeeds the next time, but also why it gives a warning: `io` is still not there.

However I still don't understand who modifies `/sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control` and how is it possible when the cgroup is not empty and would be grateful if you had an explanation for this.

But the actual issue is that podman errors are not clear at all IMHO, e.g. it doesn't show the original `EBUSY` which it must be getting when trying to modify the root `cgroup.controllers` and doesn't explain that `ENOENT` which happens when trying to add `io` to `/sys/fs/cgroup/libpod_parent/cgroup.controllers` is due to this controller not being available because it couldn't be enabled.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
